topic: Control System Analysis and Model Predictive Control (MPC)
steps: Define state-space model: mc.ss(A, B, C, D)
Convert to discrete: mc.c2d(sys, sample_time)
Simulate Linear MPC: mc.lmpc(sysd, N, R, T, lambda)
Simulate Quadratic MPC: mc.qmpc(sysd, N, R, T, lambda, Umax, S, I, x0)
Generate plots: bode(sys), nyquist(sys), step(sys)
theory: MataveControl uses Linear (Simplex) or Quadratic (Hildreth's Method) programming for MPC. Hildreth's method is computationally faster in MATLAB. Integral action (I) and slack variables (S) handle steady-state error and output constraints.

topic: MPC with Kalman-Bucy Filter and Mass-Spring-Damper System
steps: Define physical parameters m, k, and b for the system.
Construct the state-space system matrix A.
Initialize MPC parameters including lambda, reference R, and initial state x0.
Simulate MPC using mc.qmpc with quadratic programming.
Plot the control signal results.

params.lambda: Regularization factor for smooth control inputs

theory: MPC implementation for microcontrollers using a Kalman-Bucy filter and integral action added to the reference vector R prior to QP optimization. Models a second-order mass-spring-damper system (m*ddx + b*dx + k*x = F) converted to first-order state-space.

topic: JSON and Binary-JSON (BJData) encoding/decoding via JSONLab
steps: Encode MATLAB data structures into JSON or binary BJData formats
Decode JSON/BJData files into MATLAB variables
Use jdict for creating portable hierarchical data containers
Apply Base64 or Blosc2 encoding for binary data compression and transmission

params.bjdata: Binary JSON format optimized for scientific data
params.base64: Encoding scheme for representing binary data in ASCII string format
params.blosc2: High-performance compressor for binary data within JSON structures
params.encodevarname/decodevarname: Functions to ensure MATLAB variable name compatibility with JSON keys

errors: Parsing mixed-type scalars arrays in bjdata - Fixed in May 2026 update
Base64 newline issues when using zmat - Fixed in Dec 2025 update
theory: JSONLab is a toolbox for converting MATLAB data structures (structs, arrays, cells) into JSON and binary-JSON formats. It enables cross-platform data exchange, supports hierarchical data via jdict, and provides utilities for variable name sanitization and data compression to handle large-scale engineering datasets.

topic: JSONLab toolbox for JSON/Binary-JSON encoding and decoding
steps: Install JSONLab via OS package manager (Fedora, Debian, Ubuntu, Arch) or manual download
Use savejson/loadjson for standard JSON text conversion
Use savebj/loadbj for Binary JData (BJData) processing
Use jdataencode/jdatadecode to transform MATLAB structures into JData-compliant formats
Utilize jsave/jload for portable workspace sharing
Apply {zlib,gzip,base64,lzma,lz4,zstd}encode/decode for in-memory data compression

params.savejson.m: Encodes MATLAB data structures into JSON text
params.loadjson.m: Parses JSON files or strings into MATLAB structures
params.savebj.m: Encodes MATLAB variables into binary BJData format
params.jsave.m/jload.m: Saves/loads entire workspace variables to/from JSON/BJData
params.jdataencode.m: Pre-processes MATLAB data into JData specification format
params.varargin2struct.m: Converts function input arguments to a structure

fixes: Optimized parsers updated for compatibility with MATLAB R2010 and Octave 5.2
theory: Official reference library for JData (V1 Draft-3) and BJData (V1 Draft-2) specifications. Provides portable, robust encoding/decoding of MATLAB structures. Supports JSON-Mmap and multiple compression algorithms including zlib, lz4, and zstd for high-density data storage.

topic: JSONLab v2.9.8 JSON/BJData processing and neuroimaging data handling
steps: Use savejson/loadjson with BuiltinJSON option for native MATLAB encoding/decoding
Employ jsonget/jsonset for memory-mapped reading and writing of JSON/binary files
Query data structures using jsonpath with deep-scan operators
Load .nii, .h5, .snirf, .tsv, and .csv files directly via loadjd.m
Utilize jsoncache and jdlink for on-demand download of dynamic datasets

params.BuiltinJSON: Option to utilize native MATLAB/Octave jsonencode/jsondecode
params.BJData V1 Draft 2: Updated specification setting Little-Endian as default byte order
params.Blosc2: High-performance meta-compressor for large N-D array storage
params.containers.Map: Data type used automatically when keys exceed 63 characters

fixes: Added octavezmat as a fallback for zlib/gzip compression in Octave when ZMat is absent
theory: JSONLab is the official reference library for JData and BJData specifications. It facilitates language-independent data annotation and high-performance serialization, supporting memory-mapping, JSONPath queries, and multi-format parsing for scalable neuroimaging data management.

topic: JSONLab data serialization and JSONPath syntax
steps: Use savejd/loadjd for JSON/BJData I/O.
Access deep struct levels using jsonpath with '..' operator.
Manage remote data caching with jdlink and jsoncache.
Use jsonset/jsonget for key-value manipulation.

params.maxlinklevel: Limit for recursive data link resolution.
params.automap: Auto-switch to map objects for keys > 63 characters.
params.jsonpath: Query syntax supporting deep scans and dot escaping.

errors: error_pos - triggered by non-existent input files.
theory: JSONLab facilitates JSON/BJData/HDF5 serialization in MATLAB/Octave. It supports BJData Draft-2, JSONPath for deep-level structure queries, and automated dictionary/Map conversion for compatibility with long struct field names.

steps: Install Octave via conda on macOS to prevent installation hangs
Use {:} to expand varargin for function arguments
Save JSON with UTF-8 encoding to ensure character compatibility
Use zstdencode/zstddecode for Zstandard compression via ZMat
Link binary jdata files directly inside JSON objects

params.NO_ZMAT: Flag to disable ZMat compression library and use fallbacks
params.EmptyArrayAsNull: Option to toggle encoding of empty arrays as null
params.blosc2: Codec support for high-performance data compression
params.pmat: Portable MAT file format (renamed from jamm)

errors: Octave 6.4+ regexp non-utf8 error - Replace regexp with ismember
containers.Map indentation bug - Fixed for single-element maps
fread issue - Resolved via NO_ZMAT flag logic
fixes: Speed up string encoding and decoding routines
Fix loadjd and savejd file extension matching for jbids
Implement fallback zlib/glib support on Octave using file-based zip/unzip
theory: JSONLab facilitates cross-platform data exchange between MATLAB/Octave and JSON/JData formats. It supports advanced compression (Zstd, Blosc2), portable data hashing (jdatahash), and specific handling for containers.Map, numeric cells, and UTF-8 encoding.

topic: Open Optimal Control Library (OpenOCL) for Trajectory Optimization and MPC
steps: Download toolbox from openocl.github.io
Execute 'doc ocl' for API documentation
Run 'ocl.examples.cartpole' to execute cartpole example
Run 'open ocl.examples.cartpole' to inspect example source code
theory: OpenOCL is a toolbox for Trajectory Optimization and non-linear Model Predictive Control (MPC) leveraging numerical optimization and control theory to solve optimal control problems.
OpenOCL (Open Optimal Control Library) v7.04 is a software package implemented primarily in MATLAB (99.1%) for numerical optimization and optimal control.

topic: Numerical Methods in Chemical Engineering Analysis
theory: Numerical solution of linear/nonlinear algebraic systems, ODE IVPs and BVPs, PDEs, and optimization problems using literate programming for chemical engineering applications.

errors: Error while loading - Reload the page

topic: Chemical Reaction Engineering (CRE) Simulation and Design
steps: Add 'kinetics' folder to MATLAB search path using addpath()
Select subfolder based on problem type (e.g., simulation, design, regressions)
Run scripts to solve specific reactor or kinetic problems

params.kinetics: Classes for kinetic mechanisms, thermodynamics, and transport properties
params.simulation: Codes for tubular and transient perfectly stirred reactors
params.regressions: Kinetic parameter evaluation in batch reactors
params.reactive_distillation: Semi-batch reactor coupled with reactive distillation

theory: Numerical modeling of CRE problems including kinetic mechanisms, isothermal reactor design, transient CSTR and tubular reactor simulations, and parameter estimation via regression.

topic: Numerical methods for chemical engineering problems
steps: Solve steady-state PFR and 2D tubular reactor models
Model heat transfer in furnace walls and fins
Implement Model Predictive Control (MPC) for tank reactors
Apply orthogonal collocation and discretization to differential-algebraic equations (DAEs)
Perform parameter estimation for kinetic models
Solve stiff systems like Robertson kinetics
theory: Numerical solution of ODEs, PDEs, and DAEs using discretization and orthogonal collocation; application of optimization for parameter estimation and MPC in process control.

steps: Download cvx.zip or cvx.tgz from the GitHub Releases page.
Unpack the archive into a MATLAB-accessible directory.
Execute cvx_setup.m in the MATLAB command window to configure solvers and MEX files.
Use cvx_startup.m to initialize the environment for new sessions.

params.cvx_setup.m: Script to automate solver detection and system configuration.
params.cvx_startup.m: Initializes CVX paths and environment variables.
params.SeDuMi: Built-in solver for symmetric cone optimization.
params.Shims: Interface layers for external solvers Gurobi, Mosek, and GLPK.

theory: CVX implements disciplined convex programming (DCP) to transform high-level convex optimization models into standard forms required by numerical solvers.

steps: Consult User Guide and example library in examples/ subdirectory
Review CVX Forum FAQ for model acceptance and convexity issues
Verify model adheres to Disciplined Convex Programming (DCP) framework
Address numerical scaling or solver-specific limitations
Submit bug report to issue tracker only after excluding formulation or scaling errors
errors: Non-convex model - Verify convexity via FAQ and DCP rules
DCP violation - Ensure model strictness conforms to framework
Numerical scaling - Adjust model units or solver parameters
theory: CVX implements Disciplined Convex Programming (DCP), requiring users to follow specific ruleset to guarantee problem convexity. Most user errors originate from non-convexity, DCP non-compliance, or poor numerical scaling.

steps: Consult CVX Forum before filing issues
Report bugs via GitHub issue tracker
Cite CVX in research using provided BiBTeX code
errors: Unresponsive support emails - Direct queries to CVX Forum
Closed issues - Non-bug reports are closed without response
theory: CVX is a modeling system for disciplined convex programming (DCP) that enables solving convex optimization problems within MATLAB.

steps: Clone the neqsim-matlab repository
Run 'pathNeqSim.m' to add NeqSim Java libraries to the MATLAB classpath
Access NeqSim Java APIs directly within the MATLAB environment
theory: NeqSim is a Java-based library for thermodynamic and process simulation. It integrates with MATLAB via a native Java bridge (JVM/JIT), enabling direct access to unit operations and phase behavior solvers without wrappers. Applications include CO2 transport, electrolyte systems, hydrogen projects, and flow assurance.

topic: NeqSim Java API Integration and Thermodynamic Calculations
steps: Build NeqSim JAR using 'mvn -DskipTests package' or locate prebuilt JARs.
Clone neqsimmatlab repo using 'git clone https://github.com/equinor/neqsimmatlab.git'.
Add JAR directory to MATLAB classpath using 'javaaddpath' in a loop or 'javaclasspath.txt'.
Import NeqSim packages: 'import neqsim.thermo.system.*' and 'import neqsim.thermodynamicOperations.*'.
Define system (e.g., SystemSrkEos), add components, set mixing rules, and execute flash (e.g., TPflash).

params.SystemSrkEos(T, P): Constructor for Soave-Redlich-Kwong EOS; T in K, P in bar.
params.addComponent(name, n): Adds chemical component with specified mole fraction.

theory: NeqSim is a Java-based production-grade toolbox for CO2 transport, electrolytes, and flow assurance. It interfaces with MATLAB via the JVM, allowing direct calls to Java APIs for flash calculations and unit operations.

steps: Import neqsim.thermo.system and neqsim.thermodynamicOperations packages.
Instantiate EOS system (e.g., SystemSrkEos) with temperature (K) and pressure (bar).
Add components and mole fractions using sys.addComponent.
Set mixing rules and initialize system with sys.init(0).
Execute flash calculation using ThermodynamicOperations(sys).TPflash().
Retrieve phase properties using getPhase(i) methods.

params.SystemSrkEos(T, P): Initializes Soave-Redlich-Kwong EOS at T (K) and P (bar).
params.addComponent(name, fraction): Adds component with specified mole fraction.
params.setMixingRule('classic'): Applies classic mixing rules to the EOS.
params.TPflash(): Calculates phase equilibrium at constant T and P.
params.getDensity('unit'): Returns phase density in specified units.
params.getBeta(i): Returns molar fraction of phase i.

errors: JVM Mismatch - Rebuild NeqSim if MATLAB's bundled JVM version differs from build version.
Missing JARs - Ensure all NeqSim dependencies are added to the MATLAB classpath.
theory: NeqSim is a Java-based thermodynamic library allowing MATLAB to execute complex EOS calculations, flash operations, and property estimations (density, Z-factor) through the MATLAB-Java interface.

topic: NeqSim library for fluid behavior and thermodynamics
theory: NeqSim is a thermodynamic library for calculating fluid behavior, phase equilibrium, and process simulation in oil and gas production applications.

steps: Add toolbox folder to MATLAB search path
Define graph using adjacency matrix (adj), adjacency list (adjL), or edge list (edgeL)
Convert between graph representations using conversion functions (e.g., adj2edgeL)
Calculate network metrics like average degree, clustering coefficients, or centrality
Generate synthetic networks using models like Price or Forest Fire
Visualize graph structures using drawCircGraph or dotMatrixPlot

params.adj: Square adjacency matrix where A(i,j) represents edge between nodes i and j
params.adjL: Adjacency list representation of graph connectivity
params.edgeL: Edge list (typically mx3 matrix for m edges: [node1, node2, weight])

theory: A library for graph theory and network topology analysis. Includes algorithms for pathfinding (Dijkstra, BFS), connectivity (Fiedler vector, algebraic connectivity), centrality (eigen, closeness, betweenness), and structural properties (diameter, clustering coefficient, spectrum). Supports generative models for network growth and various matrix-based graph data structures.

steps: Add the toolbox folder to the MATLAB/Octave path
Call functions directly from the command prompt
theory: Toolbox for network theory metrics including degree sequences, graph spectrum, adjacency/Laplacian matrices, connectivity tests, centrality (node betweenness), community detection (Louvain, Newman), and spanning trees.

topic: Networks Toolbox installation and function categories
steps: Add toolbox folder to path
Call functions directly from the prompt: e.g., numNodes(adj_matrix)
theory: Toolbox for network analysis covering 11 categories: basic routines, diagnostics, centralities, distances, motifs, linear algebra, modularity, graph models, and visualization.
A collection of functions for graph and network analysis, originally developed for Octave and modified for MATLAB compatibility.

Computational chemical engineering education focuses on applying numerical methods (MATLAB/Python) to Material and Energy Balances, ODEs, linear algebra, PDEs, statistics, and data visualization for process modeling.

Core computational pillars in chemical engineering include numerical methods for ODEs, PDEs, and linear algebra. Technical domains requiring modeling include chemical kinetics, reactor design, process dynamics, feedback control, and advanced thermodynamics. Statistical analysis and machine learning are integrated with laboratory measurement and unit operations for data-driven modeling.

Curriculum directory covering laboratory measurement, process safety (SAChE), molecular simulation, process operations, and computational chemistry. Key digital resources for simulations and computational problem solving include LearnChemE, CACHE (Computer Aids for Chemical Engineering), and AIChE Academy.

topic: Chemical Engineering Educational Resources and Numerical Methods
steps: Utilize ChemEng Solved for MATLAB-based problem sets
Reference MATLAB Start to Finish for undergraduate thermodynamics modeling
Apply Open ChemE numerical functions for root finding, ODE solving, and optimization
Simulate process control models using transfer functions or state space representations
Access LearnChemE for simulations and modular virtual labs
theory: Computational chemical engineering leverages numerical techniques like ODE integration, root finding, and optimization for thermodynamics, reaction kinetics, and process control. Modeling involves transfer functions, state-space representations, and equations of state for unit operation design.
Numerical techniques for chemical engineering including root finding, solving ordinary differential equations (ODEs), and optimization algorithms.

steps: Select thermodynamics model (IFC-97 Steam, NRTL, Wilson, PR, or SRK EOS)
Define unit operations (Distillation, Flash, PipeSegment, Compressors, Expanders)
Set operation specifications (Recoveries, degree of subcooling, efficiencies)
Input performance curves for rotating equipment
Solve flowsheet for convergence

params.NRTL/Wilson: Activity coefficient models for liquid phase non-ideality
params.PR/SRK EOS: Equations of State for gas/oil thermodynamic calculations
params.Efficiency/Performance curves: Input parameters for compressor and expander modeling

errors: Flash unitops failing on one-phase results - fixed in latest version
fixes: Ensure phase results are correctly handled in Flash units for single-phase convergence
theory: Rigorous thermodynamic simulation utilizing Equations of State (PR/SRK) and Activity Coefficient models (NRTL/Wilson). Supports IFC-97 steam tables and multi-stage distillation with varied specifications (recovery, subcooling). Provides modeling for ASU, natural gas separation, and refrigeration loops.

steps: Select thermodynamic model (Peng-Robinson, SRK, or IFC-97 Steam)
Load components from 400+ ChemSep database
Configure unit operations (Towers, Compressors, Heat Exchangers)
Execute two-phase flashes using inside-out method

params.IFC-97: High accuracy steam model based on IAPWS97
params.PR/SRK: Cubic Equation of State providers for vapor-liquid equilibrium
params.Pumparounds: Tower model feature for heat removal/recycle

errors: PH-solver failure - inadequate convergence in certain ranges
Critical properties - missing support for phase envelope generation
fixes: Implement Newton-based solver to improve flash calculation speed
theory: Rigorous process simulation focusing on refinery and chemical engineering applications. Utilizes inside-out flash algorithms and Cubic EOS for distillation (DeC1, DeC2, C3 splitter) and multi-sided heat exchangers (coldboxes).

steps: Use fig2svg to convert MATLAB figures to SVG format
Use svg_fix_viewbox to fix scaling issues in exported SVGs
errors: SVG files do not scale/restricted to figure size - Use svg_fix_viewbox to fix
plot2svg deprecated - Use fig2svg instead
theory: fig2svg is a toolbox for converting MATLAB/Octave figures to Scalable Vector Graphics (SVG). It provides enhanced compatibility for MATLAB graphics > 2014a and supports bar/scatter plots, legends, colorbars, and 3D plots (azimuth).

errors: Error while loading page - Reload the page

steps: Initialize problem using sosprogram
Define decision variables with sosdecvar and polynomial variables with sospolyvar
Add constraints using sosineq (inequality), soseq (equality), or sosmatrixineq
Set linear objective function via sossetobj
Execute solver using sossolve
Extract numerical solutions with sosgetsol

params.P_i(x): Multivariate polynomial constraints required to be sums of squares

theory: SOS programs are convex optimization problems that minimize linear objectives subject to polynomial constraints being sums of squares. SOSTOOLS automates the conversion of these problems into Semidefinite Programs (SDP) compatible with solvers like SeDuMi, SDPT3, and Mosek. Applications include Lyapunov stability analysis and polynomial optimization.

steps: Install MATLAB v6.0 or later
Install MATLAB Symbolic Math Toolbox (v2.1.2+ for old SOSTOOLS, current for v3.00+)
Install an SDP solver (SeDuMi, SDPT3, CSDP, SDPNAL+, CDCS, SDPA, or Mosek)
Run SOSTOOLS using MATLAB sparse matrix representation

params.SDP solver: Engine for solving semidefinite programs (e.g., SeDuMi, Mosek)
params.u_i: Feasible set variables in convex optimization

theory: Convex optimization problem with linear objective functions and convex feasible sets. Uses Sum of Squares (SOS) relaxations to solve polynomial inequalities, Lyapunov function discovery, and semialgebraic set problems via semidefinite programming.
SOSTOOLS formulates and solves sum of squares (SOS) optimization programs by applying semidefinite programming (SDP) relaxations to semialgebraic problems and polynomial minimization.

topic: Tensor Toolbox for Multidimensional Data Analysis
steps: Instantiate tensor objects from multiway arrays using tensor() or sptensor().
Convert between tensors and matrices using tenmat() or sptenmat().
Perform CP decomposition using alternating least squares (cp_als) or optimization (cp_opt).
Execute Tucker decomposition using higher-order singular value decomposition (hosvd).
Compute specialized products such as the Khatri-Rao product using khatrirao().

params.ktensor: Kruskal-format tensor defined by factor matrices and weights (CP model).
params.ttensor: Tucker-format tensor defined by a core tensor and factor matrices.
params.cp_als: Function for CP decomposition via Alternating Least Squares.
params.cp_apr: Function for CP decomposition via Alternating Poisson Regression.
params.tenmat: Object representing a tensor unfolded into a matrix.

theory: The toolbox provides classes for multidimensional arrays (tensors) and algorithms for CP and Tucker decompositions, which are extensions of SVD/PCA to higher-order data used in signal processing and data mining.

steps: unfold/vec: Convert tensors to matrices/vectors
cp_orth_als: Execute Orthogonalized Alternating Least Squares
cp_arls_lev: Perform randomized ALS via leverage score sampling
ttm: Use pagemtimes for efficient tensor-matrix multiplication

params.lambda: Scaling factors in ktensor tovec; must be 1 if not explicitly included
params.pagemtimes: Default computational method in ttm.m for speed
params.fibers: Extracts fiber-wise data from tensors or sptensors

errors: gcp_opt: Error when using stratified sampling on dense tensors
tt_sample_zeros: Input argument count check failure
ktensor/tovec: Error if lambda not provided and values != 1
fixes: Update ttm to use pagemtimes for performance
Use cp_orth_als for principled tensor decomposition
theory: Low-rank tensor decomposition using Alternating Least Squares (ALS). Enhancements include orthogonalization for stability and leverage-based randomized sampling for efficiency in large-scale data.

topic: Tensor Toolbox: CP Decomposition, Optimization, and Sparse Operations
steps: Perform randomized CP decomposition using leverage score sampling: `cp_arls_lev`.
Remove empty hyperslices in sparse tensors: `sptensor/squash`.
Reconstruct partial or downsampled Tucker tensors: `ttensor/reconstruct`.
Compute symmetric CP decompositions via Subspace Power Method: `cp_ispm`.
Execute optimization-based CP decomposition using `cp_opt` with `fmincon` or `lbfgsb` wrappers.
Calculate function and gradient for ktensors: `ktensor/fg`.

params.scale: Option in cp_opt to scale factor matrices.
params.mttkrp: Matricized tensor-times-Khatri-Rao product; optimized for sparse tensors.

errors: Negative Binomial loss bug in tt_gcp_fg_setup - Fixed in v3.4
ktensor/fg.m gradient off-by-2 bug - Fixed in v3.4
fixes: Integrated L-BFGS-B library into 'library/lbfgsb'.
Enhanced mttkrp speed by 2-3X for sparse tensors.
Updated tt_sample_zeros to use efficient ismembc.
theory: Implements CP-ARLS-LEV for randomized least squares using leverage scores for efficient sampling. Supports Generalized Canonical Polyadic (GCP) decomposition with various loss functions. Symmetric CP decomposition is handled via the Subspace Power Method (SPM).

steps: Use cp_opt for optimization-based CP decomposition
Apply cp_isym for implicit symmetric CP decomposition of higher-order moments
Execute tt_opt_adam or tt_opt_lbfgs for standardized optimization
Use scale with bsxfun for efficient tensor shifting
Invoke eig_geap for Shifted Power Method in generalized tensor eigenproblems

params.viz weight: Toggle between actual and relative weights in visualizations

errors: 0 * NaN = NaN - Fixed in cp_wopt by properly zeroing NaNs via weight tensor
fixes: sptensor/mttkrp performance optimization for >100k nonzeros
export_data formatting for sptensor and lambda values
ktensor support for vectorized construction
theory: Calculates F(M) = ||M-X||^2/||X||^2 for ktensors. Implements Implicit Symmetric CP Decomposition for symmetric ktensors and Shifted Power Method for tensor eigenproblems.

topic: Tensor Toolbox Optimization and Symmetric Decompositions
steps: Use tt_opt wrappers (adam, fminunc, lbfgs, lbfgsb) for optimization
Call eig_geap for Shifted Power Method in generalized eigenproblems
Apply cp_sym or tucker_sym for symmetric tensor decomposition
Invoke cp_arls for randomized least squares CP fitting
Visualize ktensor factors using the viz function
Export ktensor objects via export_data.m

params.sumtensor: Implicit sum of tensors without forming results

errors: tucker_sym input type - requires tensor class, not symtensor
ktensor/score zero lambda - failed on dual zero inputs
sparse complex tensors - ttt and ttm operations failed
fixes: Corrected gcp_opt default lower bound
Optimized symktensor/normalize using bsxfun
Added input validation to sptenmat constructor
Fixed ktensor/full for complex values
theory: Generalized CP (GCP_OPT) utilizes optimization wrappers for non-Gaussian data. CP-ARLS employs alternating randomized least squares to reduce computational cost. Symmetric decompositions (CP_SYM, TUCKER_SYM) leverage tensor symmetry for reduced storage and complexity. Shifted Power Method (GEAP) addresses generalized tensor eigenvalue problems.

steps: Add toolbox root directory to MATLAB path for standard functionality.
Generate test problems using create_problem or create_problem_binary.
Initialize models with create_guess.
Execute CP decomposition using cp_als or cp_apr (2nd-order optimization).
Compute tensor eigenpairs using eig_geap or eig_sshopm.
Handle data I/O for sptensor and ktensor via import_data and export_data.

params.printitn: Iteration interval for status printing in CP algorithms.
params.adaptive shift: Default setting for eig_sshopm to improve convergence.
params.fixsigns: Optional call in cp_als to standardize factor sign conventions.

errors: cp_als normalization bug - fixed in v2.5
sptensor subs type error in import_data - fixed in v2.5
ktensor arrange bug (reported by Jason Mattax) - fixed to force dense matrices
theory: Tensor decomposition suite providing CANDECOMP/PARAFAC (CP) via Alternating Least Squares (ALS) and Alternating Poisson Regression (APR). Supports sparse tensors (sptensor), Kruskal tensors (ktensor), and eigenvalue computation through Generalized Eigenproblem for Antisymmetric Products (GEAP) and Shifted Power Methods (SSHOPM). MTTKRP and Khatri-Rao products optimized for performance.

topic: Tensor Toolbox v2.4 Core Functions and Tensor Classes
steps: Generate test problems and guesses using create_problem and create_guess.
Initialize tensors with tenzeros, tenrand, or tenones using size arrays [5 4 3] or lists (5,4,3).
Compute tensor eigenpairs via Shifted Symmetric Higher-Order Power Method using sshopm.
Perform Poisson Tensor Factorization using cp_apr.
Symmetrize tensors using symmetrize and verify with issymmetric.
Execute tensor-times-same-vector operations using ttsv.
Import/Export tensor data via import_data and export_data functions.

params.tenzeros: Creates zero tensor; syntax tenzeros(N*ones(1,M)) for size N and order M.
params.ttsv: Computes tensor times the same vector in every mode.
params.score: Computes factor match score for two ktensors.
params.redistribute: Moves weights from lambda into a specified mode of a ktensor.

errors: tenzeros(M,N) deprecated - Use tenzeros(N*ones(1,M)) for multidimensional zeros.
Empty tensor bugs - Fixed in tensor constructor, permute, collapse, and tenones.
Negative norm - Fixed in ktensor/norm using max(0,val).
theory: Provides classes for dense (tensor), sparse (sptensor), Kruskal (ktensor), and Tucker (ttensor) formats. Supports CP decomposition via ALS, OPT, and Poisson regression (CP_APR). Includes methods for symmetric tensor eigenpairs (SSHOPM).

topic: Tensor Toolbox Algorithm Updates and Class Methods
steps: Create mth-order tensor of size n in every mode: tenzeros(m,n)
Compute CP/weighted CP via optimization: cp_opt(X,R) or cp_wopt(X,W,R)
Normalize ktensor factors: normalize(K)
Extract ktensor components: extract(K, subset)
Rearrange ktensor components: arrange(K, permutation)
Convert ktensor to cell array: tocell(K)
Run Memory Efficient Tucker: tucker_me(X,R)

params.tenzeros(m,n): m = order, n = size for all modes
params.cp_als: Supports parameter-value pairs; calculates Unew = (Y \ Unew')'
params.normalize: Normalizes factor matrix columns to length 1 and absorbs weights into lambda
params.ncomponents: Returns number of rank-one components in a ktensor

errors: Negative values from small errors - Handled via max(0,val)
Empty tensor errors in permute/spmatrix/double - Added empty checks
tt_subscheck errors - Replaced ~isnan/~isinf with isfinite
Matlab 7.8 compatibility - Replaced '~' with 'junk' in sptensor
theory: Provides functional updates for ktensor, ttensor, and sptensor classes. Implements Alternating Least Squares (ALS) and optimization-based (CP-OPT) methods for tensor decomposition. MET package enables Memory Efficient Tucker decompositions.

topic: Tensor Toolbox updates and core functional improvements
steps: Call cp_als for Alternating Least Squares CP decomposition (replaces parafac_als)
Use cp_nmu for nonnegative tensor factorization via multiplicative updates
Apply reshape and spmatrix for tensor dimension and structure modification
Use find on tensors to return column vector indices of elements
Perform scalar division using ldivide or rdivide on tensors/sptensors

params.n: Iteration interval for printing cp_als status

errors: transpose/ctranspose - Throws error (not supported for tensors)
Subscripts out of range - Fixed via constructor input validation
Complex value insertion - Fixed in sptensor subsasgn
log(1) nonzeros - Fixed in elemfun to remove zeroed entries
fixes: Fixed squeeze and collapse for empty or zero-element sptensors
Optimized Tucker residual calculation efficiency
Standardized find function to return column vectors for tensors
Enabled cp_als to handle R=1 cases
theory: Provides dense (tensor) and sparse (sptensor) classes for multidimensional arrays. Supports CP decomposition via ALS or Multiplicative Updates (Lee & Seung) and Tucker decomposition. Elements support scalar-tensor arithmetic but not traditional transposition.

topic: Tensor Toolbox classes and functional updates
steps: Use 'tensor' for dense multi-way arrays
Use 'sptensor' for sparse multi-way arrays
Use 'ktensor' for Kruskal/CP decomposition formats
Use 'ttensor' for Tucker decomposition formats
Map tensors to matrices using 'tenmat'
Perform element-wise multi-array operations via 'tenfun'
Compute tensor-tensor products using 'ttt' and tensor-matrix products via 'ttm'
Calculate inner products and norms using 'innerprod' and 'norm'

params.tenfun: Applies specified functions to tensor elements
params.nvecs: Computes leading eigenvectors of tensor unfoldings

errors: Subscript confusion - Fixed index 58 vs ':' character bug
Dense-sparse mismatch - Fixed tenfun relational operation errors
Empty tensor calls - Fixed failures in full() and subsref
Dimension mismatch - Added size checks to innerprod
fixes: Improved nvecs efficiency using MATLAB sparse eigs
Optimized ttt via major functional overhaul
Subscripted assignment now supports dimension growth and scalar expansion
Logical operators on sptensors now consistent with sparse matrix behavior
theory: The toolbox implements multi-linear algebra by extending MATLAB's 2D sparse/dense capabilities to N-way arrays. It provides specialized classes for decomposed formats (CP/Tucker) to enable efficient computation and storage of high-dimensional data.

steps: Represent sparse data using sptensor and sptenmat classes
Model tensors using ktensor (CP) or ttensor (Tucker) representations
Perform CANDECOMP/PARAFAC or Tucker decompositions via ALS algorithms
Execute tensor operations: collapse, contract, innerprod, mttkrp, nvecs, scale
theory: Tensor Toolbox enables efficient multidimensional array operations. It supports sparse tensor objects and provides Alternating Least Squares (ALS) methods for CANDECOMP/PARAFAC and Tucker decompositions.

steps: Install only on personally owned or leased computers
Limit usage to maximum two concurrent devices (real or virtual)
Delete software and license keys before hardware disposal or leaving MIT
theory: MIT student license permits non-profit educational use restricted to the registered individual on personal hardware, governed by a two-device activation limit and termination upon graduation.

steps: Students: Access for student-owned equipment at no cost
Faculty/Staff: Obtain via departmental funds for Cornell-owned equipment
theory: Numerical computing environment and programming language. Suite includes specialized toolboxes for optimization, control systems, signal processing, and biological modeling (SimBiology).

steps: Order license via Cornell Software Licensing page.
Register MathWorks account using NetID@cornell.edu.
Download installer from MathWorks or request local server link from CU Software Licensing.

params.Single-user license: One Cornell-owned machine + one personal/home machine for assigned users.
params.Network license: Concurrent use on subnet-connected machines via designated license server.
params.Student license: Free; personal property only; installation on Cornell-owned hardware prohibited.
params.License Cycle: Annual term from August 1 to July 31.

theory: MATLAB toolboxes (e.g., Simulink, Statistics, Symbolic Math) extend core computation. Licensing models partition access by hardware ownership (Institutional vs. Student) and deployment (Single-user vs. Networked concurrent).

steps: Download software directly from MathWorks or Cornell local server.
Contact CU Software Licensing for local link if vendor download is impractical.
Specify release version and OS (Windows, macOS, or Linux) when requesting.

params.OS: Target operating system for the installer.

theory: Locally-hosted Cornell installers include all licensed components in a single, large file. Standard installs are typically sourced from MathWorks.

steps: Access toolboxes via Campus License Server
Retry access if licenses are fully utilized
Email itap-licensing@purdue.edu for exact license counts
errors: Licenses unavailable - Wait for license release from another user
theory: Purdue University manages MATLAB toolboxes through a network license server with fixed concurrent seat limits categorized by availability tiers.

steps: Identify device type (Managed vs. Individual)
Access installer via Self Service (Mac), Software Center (Windows), or MathWorks site
Authenticate using University Single Sign-on (SSO)
Download and install for Linux, Mac, or Windows
theory: A proprietary high-level multi-paradigm programming language and environment optimized for numeric computing and computationally intensive tasks.

steps: Access MATLAB portal using Single Sign-on (SSO)
Click Sign In on the MathWorks site
Enter University credentials
Download and install via the provided installer link
theory: Institutional license providing full MATLAB access on Linux, Mac, and Windows for teaching, learning, and research through federated authentication.

steps: Access MATLAB at K-State computer labs (Hale, Cardwell, Union, etc.)
Use remote access for Calvin 306/318 or Dickens 001
Acquire individual licenses via Mathworks website if campus seats are unavailable

params.Toolbox Seats: 5 seats each for Simulink, Control System, Neural Network, Optimization, Robust Control, Simulink Control Design, Signal Processing, and Symbolic Math

theory: Kansas State University provides shared concurrent MATLAB licenses for academic and research use across specific physical and remote computer labs.
license: Educational use only - Kansas State specific

steps: Register a MathWorks account using Duke credentials
Download software directly from MathWorks website
Install on personal devices (students) or Duke-owned/personal devices (faculty/staff)

params.Total Academic Headcount (TAH): Faculty/Staff license for Duke-owned/leased and personal computers
params.Total Student Headcount: Student license for personal computers (max 2 simultaneous machines)

fixes: Contact support@mathworks.com for technical assistance
Call (508) 647-7000 for MathWorks phone support
theory: Duke provides MATLAB, Simulink, and specialized toolboxes (R2025a) for noncommercial academic use. Licenses are restricted to faculty/staff (research/teaching) and students (coursework/study) within North America.

R2025a license provides access to computational tools essential for chemical engineering, including Control System, Curve Fitting, Optimization, Partial Differential Equation, Simscape (Fluids/Electrical), and Statistics and Machine Learning toolboxes.
license: Educational use only - Duke specific (Valid through July 2026)

steps: Generate Temperature-Composition (T-x-y) diagrams
Apply splines for data smoothing
Implement functions for iterative calculations
Use Newton's Method for nonlinear solving
Construct Enthalpy-Concentration (H-x-y) plots
Calculate equilibrium tie lines
theory: Numerical modeling of chemical separations using equilibrium data interpolation, nonlinear root-finding, and thermodynamic phase diagrams.

steps: Enter statements at the command line to execute instructions.
Define variables to store data in the Workspace.
Call functions by enclosing input arguments in parentheses.
Use indexing to access specific elements of an array or matrix.
Create scripts to execute sequences of commands.
Utilize graphics functions for 2-D and 3-D data visualization.

params.Matrix: A two-dimensional array used for linear algebra.
params.Array: The primary data structure in MATLAB, can be multidimensional.
params.Script: A program file containing a sequence of commands and function calls.
params.Workspace: The memory area containing variables created or imported into MATLAB.

theory: MATLAB is a matrix-based computational language. All variables are arrays, and the environment supports linear algebra, data visualization, and algorithm deployment across clusters or clouds.

topic: Solving Stiff ODEs and DAEs with ode15s
steps: Define ODE function handle: odefun = @(t,y) ...
Specify time interval tspan = [t0 tf] and initial conditions y0
Optional: Create settings using odeset (e.g., 'RelTol', 'AbsTol', 'Mass', 'Events')
Execute solver: [t,y] = ode15s(odefun, tspan, y0, options)
For continuous evaluation, use sol = ode15s(...) then deval(sol, tq)

params.odefun: Function handle for system y' = f(t,y) or M(t,y)y' = f(t,y)
params.t: Output column vector of time points
params.y: Output array of solutions at each time step
params.te, ye, ie: Event output: time, solution, and index of triggered event
params.sol: Structure for solution evaluation via deval

theory: Variable-step, variable-order (orders 1–5) multistep solver based on Numerical Differentiation Formulas (NDFs) or Backward Differentiation Formulas (BDFs/Gear's method). Optimized for stiff systems and Differential-Algebraic Equations (DAEs) where ode45 is inefficient.

steps: Identify stiffness if standard solvers are inefficient
Use specialized solvers for Index-1 differential-algebraic equations (DAEs)
theory: Numerical integration of stiff systems and Index-1 DAEs requiring variable-order methods (e.g., ode15s) for computational efficiency.

steps: Define odefun as function handle y' = f(t,y).
Specify tspan [t0 tf] and initial conditions y0.
Optional: Define options (tolerances, mass matrix, events) using odeset.
Execute [t,y] = ode45(odefun, tspan, y0, options).
Use deval with sol output for continuous evaluation.

params.odefun: Function handle for system of ODEs.
params.options: Structure from odeset (AbsTol, RelTol, Mass, Events).
params.y: Solution array; rows correspond to times in t.
params.te, ye, ie: Event times, solutions at events, and event indices.

errors: Stiff problems - Use alternative solvers like ode15s if ode45 is inefficient.
theory: Medium-order explicit Runge-Kutta (4,5) Dormand-Prince single-step solver. Solves y' = f(t,y) or M(t,y)y' = f(t,y). Recommended as first-choice solver for nonstiff systems.
Single-step solver (Dormand-Prince formula) that computes y(tn) using only the solution at the immediately preceding time point y(tn-1).

steps: Define system as first-order ODE function dy/dt = f(t,y)
For complex ODEs, manually split into real and imaginary components or use SeparateComplexParts
Identify stiffness: use ode45 for nonstiff; use ode15s for stiff or DAEs
Define tspan and initial conditions y0
Execute solver: [t,y] = solver(@odefun, tspan, y0, options)

params.ode45: Nonstiff Runge-Kutta; first choice for most problems
params.ode15s: Variable order stiff solver (BDFs); use if ode45 fails or is slow
params.ode15i: Solver for fully implicit ODEs and DAEs f(t,y,y') = 0
params.Jacobian: Analytical Jacobian to improve stiff solver performance
params.Mass: Mass matrix for solving DAEs or differential-algebraic systems
params.Events: Locates points where the solution meets specific conditions

theory: Numerical integration for IVPs. Stiff systems involve widely varying timescales, requiring implicit methods (ode15s, ode23s) to maintain stability without excessively small step sizes. Nonstiff problems use explicit methods (ode45). DAEs are solved using solvers supporting a Mass matrix.

steps: Select solver based on stiffness (ode45 non-stiff, ode15s stiff)
Configure tolerances and events using odeset
Execute solver (e.g., ode45(f, tspan, y0, options))
Extend integration range using odextend

params.NonNegative: Constraints solution components to be non-negative
params.Events: Function to detect discontinuities or terminations
params.OutputFcn: Function called after each successful time step
params.Jacobian: Jacobian matrix for stiff system performance (ode15s)

theory: ode45 is a variable-step Runge-Kutta solver for non-stiff systems (e.g., orbitode, rigidode). ode15s is a variable-order solver for stiff systems (e.g., vdpode). odeset manages solver options; odextend continues previous solutions.

topic: Troubleshooting ODE Solvers and Tolerance Settings
steps: Configure solver options using odeset.
Use ode15s, ode23s, ode23t, or ode23tb for stiff problems.
Define JPattern for sparse Jacobian problems to improve efficiency.
Use odextend to continue simulations past the final time point.
Adjust Refine parameter to increase output resolution without reducing step size.

params.RelTol: Relative error tolerance; controls significant digits.
params.AbsTol: Absolute error tolerance; threshold for components near zero.
params.InitialStep: Suggested initial step size for integration.
params.Refine: Integer factor to increase the number of output points.
params.JPattern: Sparsity pattern of the Jacobian matrix for stiff solvers.
params.OutputFcn: Function called by solver after each successful time step.

errors: Accuracy issues - Decrease RelTol/AbsTol.
Slow performance on stiff systems - Switch from ode45 to ode15s/ode23s.
Integration failure at start - Decrease InitialStep.
fixes: Set RelTol to 1e-3 (default) or smaller (e.g., 1e-6) for higher precision.
Pass tspan as [t0 tf] or a vector of specific time points.
Ensure tspan directionality: t0 > tf for backward integration.
theory: Error control is governed by |e(i)| <= max(RelTol*abs(y(i)), AbsTol(i)). Stiff equations require implicit solvers to maintain stability when the step size is limited by stability rather than accuracy.
